topic: should we implement streaming speech bubbles for the congress page, or ship it as-is and iterate?

rounds.response: Ship it as-is. Streaming speech bubbles sound appealing but they're a UI layer with non-trivial state complexity — you're introducing synchronization between stream chunks, render timing, and bubble layout, all before you even know if users *want* that interaction pattern. Get the page in front of real users first; if static bubbles feel dead, *then* you have evidence worth building against. Premature polish is just technical debt with better branding.

Ship it as-is. Streaming speech bubbles sound cool but they're scope creep until you've validated that users actually *want* to read what congress members are saying in real-time — which, historically, they don't. You don't know what breaks until it's in front of real users, and you can't iterate on a feature that hasn't shipped. The only thing streaming adds right now is complexity and delay.

Ship it as-is. Streaming speech bubbles sound delightful in theory, but they add perceived complexity to an already unfamiliar interaction pattern — users need to understand *what Congress even is* before we layer animation on top of it. Get real people using it, watch where they get confused, *then* decide if the stutter of streaming adds drama or just noise. Iteration beats speculation every time.

Ship it as-is. Streaming speech bubbles are a polish concern, not a correctness concern — and you don't yet know if the underlying debate mechanic is worth polishing. The cardinal sin of product work is optimizing something you haven't validated. Get the congress page in front of users, see if the debates themselves land, *then* invest in the presentation layer. Iteration is only weakness if you're iterating on the wrong thing; here, shipping first is the disciplined move.

verdict: Ship it as-is. Streaming speech bubbles are a polish concern, not a correctness concern — and you don't yet know if the underlying debate mechanic is worth polishing.
